questions.prompt: Which of the following are valid economic motives for a firm to hedge (vs leaving the exposure unhedged)?
choices: Reducing expected taxes via income smoothing (tax convexity)
Reducing expected costs of financial distress
Stabilizing internal cash for funding positive-NPV investments without going to capital markets
Making the firm worth more than the sum of its parts via MM I
questions.explanation: Tax convexity, distress avoidance, and investment-policy stability are the three financial-friction motives. MM I says hedging is irrelevant in a frictionless market — so violating it doesn't motivate hedging.

questions.prompt: An airline using a 12-month forward contract on jet fuel at $2.00/gallon for next year's purchases is:
choices: Hedging — locking in next year's cost regardless of where the spot price goes
questions.explanation: Forward locks in price. The firm gives up both upside (if oil falls) and downside (if oil rises). That's hedging — insurance against price moves, not a speculative bet.

questions.prompt: Key difference between hedging via FORWARD and via at-the-money CALL OPTION:
choices: Forward costs premium up front; option doesn't
Forward gives the firm a chance to profit if prices fall but caps losses if they rise
Option costs premium up front; forward doesn't, but option preserves upside if prices move favorably
questions.explanation: Forwards lock in (no upside, no downside, no premium). Options cap downside while preserving upside, but cost premium. Choice depends on whether asymmetric coverage is worth the premium.

questions.prompt: Which of the following are common patterns of BAD hedging?
choices: Speculation disguised as hedging (large directional bets unmatched to real exposures)
Hedging an exposure the firm doesn't actually have
Hedging an actual exposure to a level that costs more than the expected distress reduction
Hedging the same exposure with both forwards and futures
questions.explanation: Speculation disguised, hedging phantom exposures, and over-hedging away NPV are the three classic abuses. Using both forwards AND futures for the same exposure is unusual but not inherently bad.
